bytes.topic: MLOps Philosophy — why reproducibility, versioning, and automation matter in ML
bytes.title: DVC's MLOps Trinity — Reproducibility, Versioning, and Automation Done Right
bytes.body: ML experiments fail silently in production when datasets drift, model weights get overwritten, or pipelines run inconsistently across machines. DVC addresses all three failure modes at once. Reproducibility is enforced via `dvc repro`, which executes only the stages whose dependencies (tracked by MD5 hash in `dvc.lock`) have changed — if your `featurize` stage input hasn't changed, it won't rerun. Data versioning works like Git for large files: `dvc add data/train.csv` pushes bytes to a remote (S3, GCS, Azure Blob) while committing a `.dvc` pointer to Git, giving you `git checkout v1.2` semantics for terabyte-scale datasets. Automation is wired through `dvc.yaml` pipeline stages with explicit `deps` and `outs` — making the DAG machine-readable and CI-friendly. In GitHub Actions, `dvc pull` + `dvc repro` + `dvc push` gives you a fully auditable, cache-aware ML pipeline. Every experiment becomes a Git commit; every artifact has a lineage. That's the contract DVC enforces.
bytes.code_snippet:
# dvc.yaml
stages:
  featurize:
    cmd: python src/featurize.py
    deps:
      - data/raw.csv
      - src/featurize.py
    outs:
      - data/features.pkl
  train:
    cmd: python src/train.py
    deps:
      - data/features.pkl
      - src/train.py
    outs:
      - models/model.pkl
    metrics:
      - metrics/eval.json:
          cache: false

# Run only changed stages, cache hits skipped automatically
# $ dvc repro

bytes.topic: Experiment Tracking — logging metrics, params, artifacts, comparing runs
bytes.title: DVC Experiments — Tracking Metrics, Params, and Artifacts Without the Overhead
bytes.body: DVC's experiment tracking is built around `dvc.yaml` and `params.yaml`. Define your pipeline stages with `metrics` and `params` fields, and DVC automatically versions them alongside your code and data. Use `dvc.api.params_show()` to read params inside training scripts rather than hardcoding — this keeps runs reproducible. Log scalar metrics with `dvclive`: call `live.log_metric()` during training and `live.next_step()` per epoch; DVCLive writes to `dvclive/metric_history.tsv` and syncs with `dvc exp run`. Run experiments without committing: `dvc exp run --set-param train.lr=0.01 --set-param train.epochs=50`. Queue multiple runs with `--queue` and execute them with `dvc exp run --run-all`. Compare results directly in terminal: `dvc exp show` renders a table with all tracked metrics and params across runs, and `dvc exp diff exp-abc exp-def` diffs two specific experiments. Promote a winning experiment to a Git commit with `dvc exp branch <exp-name> <branch-name>` — no manual cherry-picking. Artifacts (models, datasets) tracked in `dvc.yaml` outputs are automatically associated with each experiment via content-addressed cache.
bytes.code_snippet:
# params.yaml
train:
  lr: 0.001
  epochs: 30
  batch_size: 64

# train.py
import dvc.api
from dvclive import Live

params = dvc.api.params_show()["train"]

with Live(save_dvc_exp=True) as live:
    for epoch in range(params["epochs"]):
        loss, acc = run_epoch(params["lr"], params["batch_size"])
        live.log_metric("loss", loss)
        live.log_metric("accuracy", acc)
        live.next_step()

# CLI
# dvc exp run --set-param train.lr=0.01 --set-param train.batch_size=128
# dvc exp show
# dvc exp diff

bytes.topic: MLflow — tracking server, model registry, projects, model serving
bytes.title: DVC + MLflow — Bridging Pipeline Reproducibility with Experiment Tracking
bytes.body: DVC handles data versioning and pipeline reproducibility; MLflow handles experiment tracking and model registry. DVCLive bridges them natively. When you run a DVC stage, DVCLive can mirror all `log_metric`, `log_param`, and `log_artifact` calls to an MLflow tracking server by setting `MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI` and enabling the MLflow logger in your `Live()` context. Each `dvc exp run` becomes an MLflow run — params from `dvc.yaml` params block map to MLflow params, and `live.log_metric()` calls populate MLflow's metric history. For model registry, after training completes inside a DVC stage, use `mlflow.register_model()` pointing at the artifact logged during the run. This means `dvc repro` drives reproducibility (locked deps, cached outputs, git-tracked params) while MLflow gives your team a central UI for comparing runs across branches. Crucially, DVC experiment commits (`--save-dvc-exp`) and MLflow run IDs stay correlated — you can trace any MLflow run back to an exact git ref and DVC cache state.
bytes.code_snippet:
# dvc.yaml stage — DVCLive mirrors to MLflow automatically
stages:
  train:
    cmd: python train.py
    deps: [data/train.csv]
    params: [params.yaml]
    metrics: [dvclive/metrics.json]
    outs: [models/]

# train.py
import os
from dvclive import Live
import mlflow

os.environ["MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI"] = "http://localhost:5000"

with Live(save_dvc_exp=True) as live:
    live.log_param("learning_rate", 0.001)
    for epoch in range(epochs):
        live.log_metric("loss", compute_loss())
        live.next_step()
    mlflow.register_model(f"runs:/{live.run.info.run_id}/model", "MyModel")

bytes.topic: Weights & Biases — experiment tracking, sweeps for hyperparameter search, reports
bytes.title: DVC + Weights & Biases — Experiment Tracking, Sweeps, and Reports in One Pipeline
bytes.body:
DVCLive bridges DVC's reproducibility model with W&B's visualization layer. Pass `report="wandb"` to `Live()` and every `live.log_metric()` call syncs to your W&B run automatically — no manual `wandb.log()` needed. DVC still owns your data versioning and pipeline DAG; W&B owns the dashboards.

For hyperparameter sweeps, define a `sweep.yaml` with W&B's Bayes or grid method, then point `program` at a shell script that calls `dvc exp run --set-param train.lr=${lr}`. The sweep agent spawns workers, each invoking a fully reproducible DVC pipeline stage. Every run is git-committed as a `dvc exp` branch — you get W&B's sweep charts *and* DVC's experiment branching.

Reports pull from W&B run history across your sweep, letting you correlate `params.yaml` values against tracked metrics. Since DVCLive auto-tags runs with the DVC commit hash, you can trace any W&B report data point back to an exact `dvc exp show` row. Use `dvc exp push origin` to share the winning run's cached artifacts alongside the W&B report URL.
bytes.code_snippet:
# train.py — DVCLive auto-syncs to W&B
from dvclive import Live
import yaml

with open("params.yaml") as f:
    params = yaml.safe_load(f)["train"]

with Live(report="wandb") as live:
    live.log_params(params)

    for epoch in range(params["epochs"]):
        loss, acc = train_one_epoch(params["lr"])
        live.log_metric("loss", loss)
        live.log_metric("accuracy", acc)
        live.next_step()  # commits step to W&B + dvclive/metric_history.tsv

bytes.topic: DVC — data versioning, pipeline stages, remote storage, reproducible pipelines
bytes.title: DVC foreach Stages — Parameterize Pipelines Without Copy-Paste
bytes.body:
DVC's `foreach` stage definition lets you declare a single stage template and instantiate it across multiple values — no shell loops, no duplicate stage blocks. Reference `${item}` inside the `do` block and DVC expands it into distinct named stages (`train@small`, `train@medium`, `train@large`), each tracked independently in the run-cache.

This integrates directly with remote storage. Every stage's outputs are content-addressed by input hashes. After `dvc push`, teammates run `dvc pull` to fetch only the artifacts they need. On `dvc repro`, DVC compares input checksums against the run-cache and skips any stage whose inputs haven't changed — changing `data/large` only re-runs `train@large`.

Params stay decoupled from stage logic via `params.yaml`. Modify `model.lr` and only the foreach variants dependent on that param re-execute. This makes hyperparameter sweeps first-class pipeline citizens. Configure your remote once in `.dvc/config` with `dvc remote add -d myremote s3://bucket/cache` — all content-addressed artifacts share the same backend across the whole team without data duplication.
bytes.code_snippet:
# dvc.yaml
stages:
  train:
    foreach:
      - small
      - medium
      - large
    do:
      cmd: python train.py --size ${item}
      deps:
        - data/${item}
        - train.py
      params:
        - params.yaml:
            - model.lr
            - model.epochs
      outs:
        - models/${item}/model.pkl

# Run only stages with changed inputs
# dvc repro --glob 'train@*'

bytes.topic: Model Registry — versioning models, staging vs production, approval workflows
bytes.title: DVC Model Registry — Versioning, Staging, and Promotion Workflows
bytes.body:
DVC's Model Registry lives inside your Git repo under `dvclive` or a dedicated `models/` directory registered via `dvc.yaml`. You register a model artifact using `dvc params` tracking or the GTO (Git Tag Operations) library that ships with DVC Studio. Each model version is a Git tag following the pattern `<model-name>#v<semver>` — e.g., `fraud-detector#v1.2.0`. Stages map directly to tag suffixes: `fraud-detector#v1.2.0#staging` promotes the artifact to staging, while `fraud-detector#v1.2.0#production` marks it live.

Promotion is done via `gto assign <model> --version v1.2.0 --stage production`, which pushes a Git tag. CI/CD pipelines watch for these tags using `gto check-ref` to gate deployments — no tag, no deploy. Approval workflows are enforced through standard Git branch protection: require PR review before tagging to production. `gto history <model>` gives a full audit trail of every stage transition, tied to the exact commit hash of the training run that produced the artifact.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Register a new model version
gto register fraud-detector --rev HEAD

# Promote to staging
gto assign fraud-detector --version v1.2.0 --stage staging

# Promote to production (after review)
gto assign fraud-detector --version v1.2.0 --stage production

# In CI: check if the pushed tag is a production assignment
gto check-ref $GITHUB_REF --stage production && echo "Deploy to prod"

# Full audit trail
gto history fraud-detector

bytes.topic: ONNX — cross-framework model format, export process, runtime inference
bytes.title: DVC — Versioning ONNX Model Exports as Reproducible Pipeline Stages
bytes.body: ONNX gives you a portable, runtime-agnostic model format, but without DVC, your export step is an undocumented shell command someone ran once. Wire the export directly into `dvc.yaml` as a pipeline stage, declaring your trained weights as `deps` and the `.onnx` file as an `out`. DVC hashes both, so `dvc repro` re-runs the export only when the upstream checkpoint changes. Store export hyperparameters (opset version, dynamic axes config) in `params.yaml` and reference them via `params:` — now they're tracked in `dvc.lock` alongside the artifact. Push the `.onnx` to your remote with `dvc push`, and any teammate or CI job can `dvc pull` the exact artifact tied to that Git commit without re-running training. For ONNX Runtime inference, pull the versioned artifact into your serving container the same way. This makes model format conversion a first-class, auditable step in your lineage graph rather than a one-off script, and lets you compare exported models across experiments using `dvc params diff` and `dvc metrics diff`.
bytes.code_snippet:
# dvc.yaml
stages:
  export_onnx:
    cmd: python src/export_onnx.py
    deps:
      - src/export_onnx.py
      - models/checkpoint.pt
    params:
      - params.yaml:
        - onnx.opset_version
        - onnx.dynamic_axes
    outs:
      - models/model.onnx

# params.yaml
onnx:
  opset_version: 17
  dynamic_axes: {input: [0], output: [0]}

# Reproduce only if deps/params changed
# dvc repro export_onnx
# dvc push models/model.onnx

bytes.topic: TensorRT — NVIDIA optimization, quantization, engine building, latency vs accuracy
bytes.title: dvc + TensorRT — Version Engines, Track Quantization Tradeoffs, Never Rebuild Blindly
bytes.body:
TensorRT `.engine` files are binary, GPU-architecture-specific, and expensive to rebuild — making them ideal DVC artifacts. Define your engine build as a `dvc.yaml` stage with `params.yaml` controlling precision (`fp32`/`fp16`/`int8`), `max_workspace_gb`, and `batch_size`. DVC hashes the ONNX source and params together, so the engine only rebuilds when inputs actually change.

Capture latency (p99 ms) and accuracy (mAP or top-1) into `metrics.json` from your TRT benchmark script. Run `dvc exp run --set-param precision=int8` to sweep quantization levels without touching code. `dvc metrics diff` then surfaces the latency/accuracy tradeoff across experiments side-by-side.

INT8 calibration datasets must be a `deps` entry — DVC invalidates the engine cache automatically when the calibration data changes. Push multi-GB `.engine` files to S3/GCS remote storage; teammates run `dvc pull` to get pre-built engines instead of a 20-minute local rebuild.

`dvc exp show --sort-by metrics.json:latency_p99` immediately surfaces your Pareto-optimal precision config across all recorded runs.
bytes.code_snippet:
# dvc.yaml
stages:
  build_engine:
    cmd: python build_engine.py
    deps:
      - model.onnx
      - calibration_data/
      - build_engine.py
    params:
      - precision
      - max_workspace_gb
      - batch_size
    outs:
      - model.engine
  benchmark:
    cmd: python benchmark.py --engine model.engine --out metrics.json
    deps:
      - model.engine
    metrics:
      - metrics.json:
          cache: false

# params.yaml
precision: fp16
max_workspace_gb: 4
batch_size: 8

# Sweep quantization — compare latency vs accuracy
# dvc exp run --set-param precision=int8
# dvc exp run --set-param precision=fp32
# dvc exp show --sort-by metrics.json:latency_p99

bytes.topic: Ray — distributed training, Ray Tune for HPO, Ray Serve for model serving
bytes.title: DVC + Ray — Versioned HPO Pipelines and Artifact-Gated Serving
bytes.body:
DVC pipelines wrap Ray Tune HPO runs cleanly: define a `dvc.yaml` stage whose `cmd` calls your Ray script, declare `params.yaml` keys under `params:`, and point `metrics:` at DVCLive's output directory. When you run `dvc exp run`, DVC feeds your versioned dataset to Ray's distributed workers and captures every trial's metrics through `dvclive`.

Inside each trial, instantiate `Live()` and call `live.log_metric()` + `live.next_step()` per epoch. DVCLive writes per-step metric files that DVC's experiment tracker surfaces in `dvc exp show`. After `Tuner.fit()` completes, the best checkpoint becomes a tracked `outs:` artifact in your stage — `dvc push` syncs it to remote storage.

For Ray Serve, add a downstream `dvc.yaml` stage that lists that model artifact under `deps:`. Your serving script calls `dvc pull` to hydrate the checkpoint before invoking `serve.run()`. The DAG ensures the serve stage only re-executes when the upstream model's content hash changes — no stale deployments, full lineage from raw data to live endpoint.
bytes.code_snippet:
# train_ray.py — invoked by dvc repro via dvc.yaml
import yaml
from ray import tune
from dvclive import Live

with open("params.yaml") as f:
    cfg = yaml.safe_load(f)["ray"]

def trial(config):
    with Live() as live:
        loss = train_model(config["lr"], config["depth"])
        live.log_metric("val_loss", loss)
    tune.report({"val_loss": loss})

tuner = tune.Tuner(
    trial,
    param_space={"lr": tune.loguniform(1e-4, 0.1), "depth": tune.randint(2, 8)},
    tune_config=tune.TuneConfig(num_samples=cfg["num_samples"]),
)
tuner.fit()

bytes.topic: Hyperparameter Optimization — grid vs random vs Bayesian search, early stopping
bytes.title: DVC Hyperparameter Search — Grid, Random, and Bayesian with dvc exp
bytes.body:
DVC's experiment engine treats each hyperparameter run as a versioned, reproducible unit. Grid search is the bluntest tool: queue every combination with `dvc exp run --queue --set-param 'train.lr=0.001,0.01,0.1' --set-param 'train.batch_size=32,64'`, then `dvc queue start --jobs 4` for parallel execution. Every result is tracked in `.dvc/tmp/exps` and comparable via `dvc exp show`.

Random search cuts cost by sampling a budget of trials instead of exhausting the grid — same CLI, just fewer `--set-param` values chosen stochastically in your orchestration script.

For Bayesian search, pair DVC with Optuna and DVCLive. Each `trial` callback logs params and metrics via `live.log_param` / `live.log_metric`, and Optuna's TPE sampler uses those results to propose better candidates. DVCLive's `step` API doubles as your early-stopping hook: emit `live.log_metric('val_loss', val)` each epoch and check `trial.should_prune()` — Optuna's MedianPruner halts unpromising runs before they waste compute. Compare all survivors with `dvc exp show --sort-by metrics.val_loss --num 10`.
bytes.code_snippet:
import optuna
from dvclive import Live

def objective(trial):
    lr = trial.suggest_float("lr", 1e-4, 1e-1, log=True)
    depth = trial.suggest_int("depth", 3, 8)

    with Live(dir="dvclive", save_dvc_exp=True) as live:
        live.log_param("lr", lr)
        live.log_param("depth", depth)

        for epoch, val_loss in train(lr=lr, depth=depth):
            live.log_metric("val_loss", val_loss)
            live.next_step()
            if trial.should_prune():
                raise optuna.TrialPruned()

    return val_loss

study = optuna.create_study(pruner=optuna.pruners.MedianPruner())
study.optimize(objective, n_trials=40)

bytes.topic: Model Serving — REST APIs for inference, batching, latency SLAs
bytes.title: DVC + FastAPI — Serving Version-Pinned Models via dvc.api
bytes.body:
DVC's Python API lets you pull versioned model artifacts directly into a serving process without a separate model store. `dvc.api.open()` accepts a `rev` parameter — a Git tag, branch, or commit — so your inference server declares exactly which artifact it consumes. Pair this with `dvc.api.params_show()` to read `params.yaml` keys like `serve.batch_size` and `serve.latency_sla_ms` from the same config file used during training. This keeps serving behavior coupled to the experiment that produced the model, eliminating config drift.

For latency SLAs, enforce the budget at the route level: record wall-clock time around `model.predict()` and return a 503 with a `X-Latency-Budget-Exceeded` header when you breach the threshold. The batch size cap from `params.yaml` bounds worst-case inference time deterministically. When you promote a new model version via `dvc push` and retag the artifact, the server restarts against the new `rev` with zero config changes — the params file travels with the artifact in the DVC cache.
bytes.code_snippet:
import dvc.api, joblib, time
from fastapi import FastAPI, Response
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()
params = dvc.api.params_show()["serve"]

with dvc.api.open("models/classifier.pkl", rev=params["model_rev"]) as f:
    model = joblib.load(f)

class Batch(BaseModel):
    features: list[list[float]]

@app.post("/predict")
def predict(batch: Batch, response: Response):
    rows = batch.features[: params["batch_size"]]
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    preds = model.predict(rows).tolist()
    elapsed_ms = (time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000
    if elapsed_ms > params["latency_sla_ms"]:
        response.headers["X-Latency-Budget-Exceeded"] = f"{elapsed_ms:.1f}ms"
        response.status_code = 503
    return {"predictions": preds, "latency_ms": round(elapsed_ms, 2)}

bytes.topic: Model Monitoring — prediction drift, data drift, performance degradation in prod
bytes.title: DVC Model Monitoring — Catching Prediction Drift and Performance Degradation in Prod
bytes.body:
DVC's production monitoring story is built on DVCLive. Wrap each inference batch in a `Live` context to log prediction distributions and ground-truth labels as they arrive. Compute KS-statistic between your reference distribution (snapshotted at training time with `np.save`) and live predictions using `scipy.stats.ks_2samp`, then push that value with `live.log_metric('ks_stat', ks_stat)`. DVCLive persists metrics to `dvc.yaml`-compatible JSON, so `dvc metrics diff baseline HEAD` gives a clean drift delta across any two Git refs — no separate dashboard required.

For data drift, log per-feature statistics (mean, std, null rate) as metrics each batch. Wire monitoring as a downstream DVC pipeline stage: if `ks_stat > 0.1`, the stage exits non-zero, blocking automated retraining triggers. Use `live.log_sklearn_plot('calibration_curve', y_true, y_prob)` to surface probability degradation before raw accuracy drops. Run `dvc plots diff prod-week-1 prod-week-4` to overlay calibration curves across rolling windows — a leading indicator of silent model rot before SLA breaches surface in dashboards.
bytes.code_snippet:
import numpy as np
from dvclive import Live
from scipy.stats import ks_2samp
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score

ref_preds = np.load("artifacts/ref_predictions.npy")
prod_preds = model.predict(X_batch)

ks_stat, p_value = ks_2samp(ref_preds, prod_preds)

with Live("monitoring", report="html") as live:
    live.log_metric("ks_stat", float(ks_stat))
    live.log_metric("drift_p_value", float(p_value))
    live.log_metric("accuracy", accuracy_score(y_batch, prod_preds))
    live.log_sklearn_plot("calibration_curve", y_batch, prod_preds)

if ks_stat > 0.1:
    raise SystemExit(1)  # Fails DVC stage, blocks retraining pipeline

bytes.topic: CI/CD for ML — automated retraining, evaluation gates, deployment pipelines
bytes.title: DVC Pipelines in CI/CD — Automated Retraining with Evaluation Gates
bytes.body:
DVC's `dvc repro` is the backbone of ML CI/CD. When a GitHub Actions workflow triggers on data or code changes, `dvc repro` reruns only the invalidated stages defined in `dvc.yaml` — skipping cached stages automatically via content-addressed storage.

The critical piece is the evaluation gate. After `dvc repro`, use `dvc metrics show` and `dvc metrics diff` to compare metrics against the baseline commit. A custom Python script or `dvc params diff` can assert thresholds — block the pipeline if `val/f1` drops below 0.85, for example.

For deployment, gate on `dvc push` only after the metric check passes. Tag the Git commit with `dvc tag` and push the model artifact to your DVC remote (S3, Azure Blob, GCS). The downstream deploy job pulls the exact artifact via `dvc pull` using the tagged commit ref, guaranteeing reproducibility.

Use `dvc studio` or parse `dvclive` logs for experiment tracking across runs — giving you a full audit trail of every retrain without storing large binaries in Git.
bytes.code_snippet:
# .github/workflows/ml-pipeline.yml (key steps)
- name: Reproduce pipeline
  run: dvc repro

- name: Check evaluation gate
  run: |
    python evaluate_gate.py --metric val/f1 --threshold 0.85
  # evaluate_gate.py reads dvc metrics show --json

- name: Push artifacts if gate passes
  run: |
    dvc push
    git tag "model-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)"
    git push --tags

- name: Deploy — pull exact artifact by tag
  run: dvc pull --run-cache

bytes.topic: Feature Stores — centralized feature management, online vs offline features
bytes.title: DVC as an Offline Feature Store — Versioned Features with dvc.api
bytes.body:
DVC doesn't ship a feature store, but its pipeline and artifact model cover the offline half cleanly. Define feature engineering as a `dvc stage` in `dvc.yaml`, declare raw data as `deps` and computed features as `outs` with `cache: true`. DVC hashes each output and syncs it to your remote (S3, GCS, Azure Blob) — every `git commit` becomes an immutable feature snapshot.

At training time, `dvc.api.open()` fetches the exact feature parquet tied to a Git revision — no 'which features did we train on?' archaeology. Pin a revision to a model experiment via `dvc exp run` and feature provenance travels with the run automatically.

For online features, DVC alone isn't sufficient — you need a low-latency serving layer (Redis, Feast, a custom FastAPI endpoint). The bridge: after `dvc pull`, export the versioned offline features and hydrate your online store during the deployment step. DVC becomes the source of truth for feature schemas and historical values; the online store handles sub-millisecond reads at inference time. This split keeps retraining reproducible without coupling it to online serving infrastructure.
bytes.code_snippet:
# dvc.yaml — offline feature pipeline
# stages:
#   build_features:
#     cmd: python src/features.py
#     deps: [data/raw/events.parquet, src/features.py]
#     outs:
#       - data/features/user_features.parquet: {cache: true}

import dvc.api
import pandas as pd

# Fetch feature snapshot pinned to a specific Git revision
with dvc.api.open(
    "data/features/user_features.parquet",
    rev="v1.3.0",  # Git tag tied to experiment
    mode="rb",
) as f:
    offline_features = pd.read_parquet(f)

# Hydrate online store (e.g. Redis) after dvc pull in CI
feature_records = offline_features.to_dict("index")

bytes.topic: Quantization & Pruning — reducing model size, int8/fp16, trade-offs
bytes.title: DVC — Versioning int8/fp16 Quantization Trade-offs as Reproducible Experiments
bytes.body: When you quantize from fp32 to int8, you're trading accuracy for size and latency. DVC makes that trade-off auditable. Define a `quantize` stage in `dvc.yaml` with your full-precision checkpoint as a dep and the quantized artifact as an out — DVC tracks the binary under content-addressed storage. Push quantization hyperparameters (dtype, calibration samples, per-channel vs per-tensor) into `params.yaml` so `dvc repro` detects config drift automatically. Log accuracy, model size in MB, and latency into a JSON metrics file; `dvc metrics diff` then shows exactly what you gave up versus the fp32 baseline. For pruning, track sparsity ratio as a param and remaining parameter count as a metric. Use `dvc exp run --set-param quant.dtype=int8` and `--set-param quant.dtype=fp16` to branch experiments without creating Git branches. `dvc exp show` renders a table comparing all variants side-by-side. The real payoff: every size/accuracy trade-off is a reproducible, diffable, shareable experiment — not a Slack message saying 'I think int8 was good enough.'
bytes.code_snippet:
# Run int8 and fp16 quantization as tracked DVC experiments
dvc exp run --set-param quant.dtype=int8 --set-param quant.calib_samples=512
dvc exp run --set-param quant.dtype=fp16 --set-param quant.calib_samples=512

# Compare accuracy vs size across all runs
dvc exp show --select quant.dtype,metrics.accuracy,metrics.model_size_mb

# Diff quantized metrics against fp32 baseline on current branch
dvc metrics diff main

# Promote the winning experiment and push quantized artifact to remote
dvc exp apply exp-3a7f92c
dvc push

bytes.topic: A/B Testing Models — shadow deployment, canary releases, champion/challenger
bytes.title: DVC Model Registry — Champion/Challenger, Shadow Deployment & Canary Releases
bytes.body:
DVC's model registry makes champion/challenger testing auditable without a separate registry service. Tag your production model as `champion` and the candidate as `challenger` using `dvc artifacts tag add`, then pull both into your serving environment independently with `dvc artifacts get`.

For shadow deployment, wire the challenger into a dedicated `dvc.yaml` stage with log-only semantics — it receives mirrored inference requests but its outputs never reach users. Emit shadow metrics via the `metrics:` key in that stage, then compare with `dvc metrics diff main exp-canary-v2` to evaluate without bias.

Canary releases map cleanly to `params.yaml`. Define a `traffic_split` float there and reference it in your serving stage command. Increment it in steps — 5 %, 20 %, 50 % — running `dvc repro` at each threshold to regenerate metric artifacts. Gate full promotion on `dvc metrics show`: if the challenger's held-out metric (e.g., F1 or NDCG) clears your threshold, a single `dvc artifacts tag add champion` atomically promotes it. Every tag move is git-backed, giving you a full rollback path via `dvc artifacts tag add champion --rev <prev-sha>`.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Tag production model and candidate in DVC model registry
dvc artifacts tag add champion models/classifier --rev main
dvc artifacts tag add challenger models/classifier --rev exp-canary-v2

# Pull both into shadow-serving environment
dvc artifacts get . models/classifier --tag champion -o models/champion/
dvc artifacts get . models/classifier --tag challenger -o models/challenger/

# Diff held-out metrics between the two git revisions
dvc metrics diff main exp-canary-v2

# Promote challenger to champion after shadow validation
dvc artifacts tag add champion models/classifier --rev exp-canary-v2

bytes.topic: Data Pipelines — feature engineering pipelines, data validation, drift detection
bytes.title: DVC Pipelines — Feature Engineering, Validation, and Drift Detection in One DAG
bytes.body: DVC pipelines let you encode your entire ML data workflow as a reproducible DAG in `dvc.yaml`. Each stage declares `deps`, `outs`, and `cmd`, so DVC tracks exactly what changed and reruns only stale stages. For feature engineering, define a `featurize` stage that reads raw data and writes transformed parquet to `data/features/`. Chain a `validate` stage that runs Great Expectations or Pandera checks against those features — if validation fails, the pipeline halts before training. For drift detection, add a `drift` stage that compares the current feature distribution against a stored baseline using `evidently` or a custom script, writing a `drift_report.json` as an out. DVC's content-addressed cache means re-running `dvc repro` on unchanged data is instant — it restores outputs from cache rather than recomputing. Use `dvc params.yaml` to version distribution thresholds, so changing a drift tolerance triggers only the `drift` stage, not the entire pipeline. Combine with `dvc metrics show` to surface validation pass rates and drift scores across experiments.
bytes.code_snippet:
stages:
  featurize:
    cmd: python src/featurize.py
    deps: [data/raw/train.csv, src/featurize.py]
    outs: [data/features/train.parquet]

  validate:
    cmd: python src/validate.py
    deps: [data/features/train.parquet, src/validate.py]
    outs: [reports/validation.json]
    metrics: [reports/validation.json]

  drift:
    cmd: python src/drift.py --threshold ${drift.threshold}
    deps: [data/features/train.parquet, data/baseline/ref.parquet]
    params: [drift.threshold]
    metrics: [reports/drift_report.json]

bytes.topic: Infrastructure — GPU cluster management, spot instances for training, cost optimization
bytes.title: DVC Checkpoints — Surviving Spot Instance Preemptions Without Losing Training Progress
bytes.body:
Spot instances can vanish mid-epoch. Without checkpointing, you lose hours of GPU time and pay again to restart. DVC's checkpoint outputs solve this at the pipeline level — not the application level.

Mark a stage output as `checkpoint: true` in `dvc.yaml`. DVC then commits that output to the DVC cache after every `Live.next_step()` call via DVCLive. If your spot instance is preempted, re-running `dvc exp run` on a fresh instance resumes from the last committed checkpoint rather than epoch zero — the cache is already warm on your remote (S3, GCS, Azure Blob).

Pair this with `dvc exp run --queue` to enqueue a sweep of hyperparameter configs before your spot fleet even launches. `dvc queue start --jobs 4` distributes them across workers. Preempted workers drop their queue item back; surviving workers pick it up. Each completed epoch is a cache hit — never recomputed.

Use `dvc exp show` to inspect which checkpoint epoch each queued experiment reached, so you prioritize restarting the runs closest to convergence rather than blindly re-queuing everything.
bytes.code_snippet:
# dvc.yaml
stages:
  train:
    cmd: python train.py
    deps:
      - data/
      - train.py
    outs:
      - checkpoints/:
          checkpoint: true

# train.py
from dvclive import Live
import torch

with Live() as live:
    model = load_or_init("checkpoints/")
    for epoch in range(params["epochs"]):
        loss = train_one_epoch(model)
        live.log_metric("loss", loss)
        torch.save(model.state_dict(), "checkpoints/model.pt")
        live.next_step()  # triggers DVC checkpoint commit
